coredata.dc:title: A nanozyme based wearable device for colorimetric monitoring of UV radiation exposure in sunlight
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.
coredata.dc:creator: ZHOU, Guofen
YU, Linlin
GUO, Wenlong
JIANG, Zhongwei
HU, Lianzhe
WANG, Min
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nanozyme is used for monitoring the dose of ultraviolet (UV) in sunlight for the first time. We find that nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) can effectively catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) under different UV region including UVA, UVB, and UVC. In comparison, the N-doped C-dots fail to show the catalytic activity under visible light. The finding is applied for distinguishing UV light and visible light through the color change of TMB. More importantly, the photo-oxidation TMB catalyzed by N-doped C-dots can work well in a near neutral pH, which is beneficial for its application in wearable device due to the reducing of acid corrosion. Finally, a wearable bracelet is fabricated by integrating N-doped C-dots and TMB into hydrogels. The bracelet is used for monitoring the UV exposure in sunlight. The color change of TMB in the bracelet can be clearly observed by naked eyes or quantified by a smartphone. The bracelet is also used for distinguishing different weather condition such as sunny/cloudy or different place such as indoor/outdoor during a sunny day. This study provides the first example of nanozyme in wearable UV sensor and will stimulate the applications of nanozyme in wearable device.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanozyme
UV
Carbon dots
Wearable device
Colorimetric

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The light-activated oxidase-like activity of N-doped C-dots
Determination of UV dose in hydrogel
The photo-catalytic activity of N-doped C-dots
The wavelength-dependent activity and mechanism discussion
Sensing the UV radiation in different weather and place
The smart wearable device for UV monitoring

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The absorption curves of 300 µM TMB without (a) and with 1 µg/mL N-doped C-dots (b) in 20 mM HEPES buffer (pH 4) after 300 nm UV irradiation. (B) The absorption curves of 300 µM TMB without (a) and with 1 µg/mL N-doped C-dots (b) in 20 mM HEPES buffer (pH 6) after 300 nm UV irradiation. Xenon lamp: 20 W/m2, 10 min.

(A) The absorption curves of 300 µM TMB and 1 µg/mL N-doped C-dots in HEPES buffer (20 mM, pH 6) after external light irradiation with different wavelength. (B) The comparison of TMB and TMB/N-doped C-dots systems after different light irradiation. After the light irradiation, the absorbance values at 652 nm was recorded for comparison. Xenon lamp: 20 W/m2, 10 min.

The absorption curves of 300 µM TMB with different nanomaterials in HEPES buffer (20 mM, pH 6) after 300 nm light irradiation (20 W/m2) for 10 min. a: blank, b: N-doped C-dots, c: imidazole modified graphene quantum dots, d: N-doped graphene, e: N-free graphene quantum dots, f: N-free C-dots. The concentrations of nanomaterials were 1 µg/mL.

(A) The absorption curves of 300 µM TMB and 1 µg/mL N-doped C-dots under after exposing at different weather conditions (sunny or cloudy) for 5 min. (B) The absorption curves of 300 µM TMB and 1 µg/mL N-doped C-dots under after exposing at different places (indoor or outdoor) for 5 min. Inset: the corresponding images taken by a smartphone.

The photo-oxidation of TMB catalyzed by N-doped C-dots in gels at different UV doses. (A) The TMB/N-doped C-dots loaded gels with different doses of UVA. (B) The TMB/N-doped C-dots loaded gels with different doses of UVB. (C) The TMB/N-doped C-dots loaded gels with different doses of UVC.
The wearable bracelet exposed outside in sunlight at a sunny day with different time. The pictures were taken by a smartphone, and the RGB values were obtained from a color recognition APP.
(A) The photo-oxidation of TMB catalyzed by N-doped C-dots. (B) The wearable bracelet exposed in sunlight, the color change can be monitored by naked eye or the smartphone.

head.ce:title: A nanozyme based wearable device for colorimetric monitoring of UV radiation exposure in sunlight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Green Synthesis and Applications, College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing 401331, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Green Synthesis and Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aChongqing Key Laboratory of Green Synthesis and Applications, College of Chemistry, Chongqing Normal University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nanozyme is used for monitoring the dose of ultraviolet (UV) in sunlight for the first time. We find that nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) can effectively catalyze the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) under different UV region including UVA, UVB, and UVC. In comparison, the N-doped C-dots fail to show the catalytic activity under visible light. The finding is applied for distinguishing UV light and visible light through the color change of TMB. More importantly, the photo-oxidation TMB catalyzed by N-doped C-dots can work well in a near neutral pH, which is beneficial for its application in wearable device due to the reducing of acid corrosion. Finally, a wearable bracelet is fabricated by integrating N-doped C-dots and TMB into hydrogels. The bracelet is used for monitoring the UV exposure in sunlight. The color change of TMB in the bracelet can be clearly observed by naked eyes or quantified by a smartphone. The bracelet is also used for distinguishing different weather condition such as sunny/cloudy or different place such as indoor/outdoor during a sunny day. This study provides the first example of nanozyme in wearable UV sensor and will stimulate the applications of nanozyme in wearable device.

A wearable device was developed for monitoring the dose of UV radiation in sunlight, based on the finding that N-doped C-dots can catalyze the oxidation of TMB under the whole UV region including UVA, UVB, and UVC.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:section.ce:para: The excessive exposure to sunlight may result in healthy problems such as skin aging, skin burning, and even skin cancers [1]. The ultraviolet (UV) radiation in sunlight is responsible for the skin-related diseases due to their relatively high energy [2–4]. As a comparison, the visible light in sunlight shows negligible effects on the skin issues. UV radiation is usually categorized into UVA (320–400 nm), UVB (280–320 nm), and UVC (100–280 nm), depending on the level of energy [5]. Due to the increasing demand for outdoor activities, various personal devices for monitoring UV radiation were developed including photoelectric devices and photochromic devices [6–10]. Recently, Zhang et al. reported an interesting UV-monitoring device based on UV-induced color change of a commercial dye 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB). This dosimetry-based device is low cost and suitable for wearable personal health equipment [11]. However, the device based on TMB can only monitor the UV light in the region of UVB and UVC, while could not be used for the sensing of UVA. Due to the ozone layer in the sky, the most of UV radiation reaching the earth's surface actually is UVA [12]. Therefore, developing more sensitive UV-responsive device that can detect full-wavelength UV radiation is important in skin protection for outdoor activities.

Nanomaterial-based sensors have attracted increasing attention in recent years [13–17]. Nanozymes are kinds of nanomaterial with enzyme-like activities [18–20], which have found wide applications in various fields including cancer therapy, immunoassay, cell imaging, environmental protection, and wearable device [21–26]. Recently, light-activated nanozymes have gained increasing interest [27]. Different from normal nanozymes, light-activated nanozymes using light as an external stimulus to regulate their activity. Light-activated nanozymes have been used in different fields such as therapy, sensing, and environmental applications [28–32]. For example, the photo-oxidation (365 nm irradiation) activity of nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-doped C-dots) has been reported and used for photodynamic antimicrobial therapy [32]. Obviously, the light-activated nanozymes provide a bridge between optics and nanozymes. However, the application of light-activated nanozymes in UV dose sensing and/or wearable device fabrication is still not reported.

Herein, the light-activated nanozyme was used for monitoring the dosimetry of UV radiation for the first time. By using TMB as the colorimetric substrate, the wavelength-dependent activity of N-doped C-dots was investigated. The N-doped C-dots showed robust activity under UV region (UVA, UVB, and UVC), while failed to show catalytic activity under the visible light. According to previous study, TMB itself could be oxidized upon UVB and UVC radiation, but could not be oxidized under UVA radiation due to the relative low energy of UVA [11]. By using of N-doped C-dots as a catalyst, the oxidation of TMB was more easily to achieved, the sensing of full-wavelength UV radiation was successfully achieved (Scheme 1A ). Due to the catalytic amplification effect, the proposed method is more sensitive toward UV radiation compared with the light-induced TMB oxidation without an extra catalyst. More importantly, a wearable bracelet was fabricated by integrating TMB and N-doped C-dots into a gel-loaded paper (Scheme 1B). Such a wearable device was used for monitoring the UV exposure in sunlight by naked eye through the color change of TMB. The color change in the bracelet can also be quantified by a smartphone with color recognition APP.

TMB and agarose were purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) was obtained from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Imidazole modified graphene quantum dots (QDs) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. N-doped graphene was obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. N-free graphene QDs was synthesized by the carbonization of citric acid [33]. N-free C-dots was prepared through the hydrothermal reaction of 3-hydrooxyphenylboric acid [34].

The UV–Vis spectra were obtained on a UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained on an FEI Tecnai G2 F30 microscope (Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained from a Escalab 250Xi electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). The light radiation experiments were performed using a CEL-HXF300-T3 xenon lamp (Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology, China) with suitable optical filter for wavelength selection. The power of light radiation was measured by a CEL-NP 2000 full-wavelength optical power meter (Zhongjiao Jinyuan Technology, China).

The N-doped C-dots were synthesized according to previous literature with little modification [35]. In brief, 15 mL NMP and 0.02 g NaOH were mixed in a round-bottom flask, and the mixture was heated and kept at 150 °C for 8 h. After the solution cooling down, the obtained N-doped C-dots were stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The light-activated oxidase-like activity of N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the light-activated oxidase-like activity of N-doped C-dots, 300 µM TMB was mixed with 1 µg/mL N-doped C-dots in 1 mL HEPES buffer (20 mM), and then the solution was exposed to a xenon lamp with different wavelengths (by using optical filter). The power of light was identified with the optical power meter. After 10 min exposing, the UV–Vis spectra were measured and the absorption intensities at 652 nm were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of UV dose in hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrogels containing TMB and N-doped C-dots were prepared as follows. First, 0.1 g agarose was added into 5 mM HEPES buffer (20 mM, pH=6), and the solution was heated to dissolve the agarose. Then 500 µL of the above solution was added into the hole of a 24-well cell culture plate. Before the solidify of agarose, TMB and N-doped C-dots were added with a final concentration of 300 µM and 1 µg/mL, respectively. Herein, the 24-well cell culture plate was used to make the gel as a specific round shape. Finally, the round-shape TMB and N-doped C-dots loaded gels were sealed and stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator.

The wearable bracelet was designed to contain two gels, the TMB/N-doped C-dots loaded gel and only TMB loaded gel. The two gels were integrated onto the paper based bracelet carrier through the transparent double-sided tape. The TMB loaded gel was used as a reference, while the TMB/N-doped C-dots loaded gel was used as an indicator. The pictures of the wearing bracelet on the arm were taken by a smartphone.

The N-doped C-dots were synthesized by using NMP as the single precursor of C and N atoms. The obtained N-doped C-dots were characterized by high-resolution TEM. As shown in Fig. S1, the N-doped C-dots were spherical shape and showed an average diameter of 2–3 nm. A lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was observed in the high resolution TEM, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphite [36]. The absorption and fluorescence spectra of N-doped C-dots were shown in Fig. S2. The N-doped C-dots showed a broad absorption band in the UV region. Excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescent emission of N-doped C-dots was also observed, which was consistent with the typical character of fluorescent C-dots. The XPS spectra of N-doped C-dots were shown in Fig. S3. The typical peaks of N 1s, C 1s, and O 1s binding energies located around 285, 400, and 532 eV. All these data suggest the successful preparation of the N-doped C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photo-catalytic activity of N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: As show in Fig. 1A , TMB itself can be photo-oxidized to generate an absorption peak around 652 nm under 300 nm UV radiation (UVB region) at a solution of pH 4. The result is consistent with previous report that TMB could be oxidized directly by UVB radiation [11]. In the presence of N-doped C-dots, the absorbance value around 652 nm was increased, indicating N-doped C-dots could accelerate the photo-oxidation reaction of TMB. The control experiment was also conducted without extra light stimulation. As shown in Fig. S4, TMB itself was not oxidized after placed in the dark for 10 min, and N-doped C-dots also could not catalyze the oxidation of TMB without light irradiation.

The effect of solution pH on the photo-oxidation of TMB catalyzed by N-doped C-dots was provided in Fig. S5. The N-doped C-dots could catalyze the photo-oxidation of TMB under a wide pH range from pH 2 to pH 6. As shown in Fig. 1B, the UVB-induced oxidation of TMB was negligible under pH 6. In the presence of N-doped C-dots, the UVB-induced oxidation was accelerated, and an intense absorption peak around 652 nm was observed. The pH condition of the colorimetric reaction is important for its further applications in wearable device. Although the oxidation of TMB induced by UVB and UVC radiation has been reported, the reaction can only occur in acidic environment (around pH 4). The near-neutral pH condition is more suitable for wearable device, as the acidic solution may be corrosive to the device. Therefore, pH 6 was selected in the further experiments including the UV dose measurement and wearable bracelet fabrication.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The wavelength-dependent activity and mechanism discussion
ce:section.ce:para: The photo-oxidation of TMB catalyzed by N-doped C-dots is highly dependent on the wavelength of light. Fig. 2 shows the absorption curves of the catalytic system under light irradiation with different wavelength. The N-doped C-dots can significantly catalyze the oxidation of TMB under 254, 300, 350, and 400 nm irradiation. In comparison, the catalytic effect is not significant from 450 to 650 nm. This suggests the method could be used for the discrimination of UV light and visible light. The comparison of photo-oxidation of TMB in the absence and presence of N-doped C-dots was shown in Fig. 2B. Consisted with previous study, the oxidation of TMB was only observed by UVB and UVC irradiation without the use of an additional catalyst. In the presence of N-doped C-dots, the oxidation of TMB could be observed in the region of UVA, UVB, and UVC. Fig. S6 shows the time-dependent absorbance changes of TMB/N-doped C-dots solution under the irradiation of 350 nm (UVA region). The results indicate the introduction of N-doped C-dots can effectively extend the wavelength-response range of the photo-oxidation of TMB.

The mechanism of UV-induced oxidation of TMB has already been demonstrated. The light-induced formation of reactive oxygen species (mainly O2 •−) is responsible for the oxidation of TMB [11]. In the presence of N-doped C-dots, photosensitized oxygen activation and accompanied O2 •− production result in the acceleration oxidation of TMB [32]. The proposed mechanism of UV-induced oxidation of TMB catalyzed by N-doped C-dots was given in Fig. S7. The According to previous study, the doping of N atom in C-dots also plays important roles in the photo-catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 3 , the photo-oxidation activities of different carbon-based nanomaterials were evaluated. Two kinds of N-doped carbon nanomaterials (imidazole modified graphene QDs and N-doped graphene) were also able to catalyze the oxidation of TMB under 300 nm UV irradiation. However, N-free graphene QDs and C-dots were failed to show similar catalytic activity. The result is consistent with the previous literature that doping elements with electron donor properties can significantly increase the photo-catalytic activity of fluorescent C-dots [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing the UV radiation in different weather and place
ce:section.ce:para: The N-doped C-dots/TMB system was successfully used for monitoring the UV radiation in sunlight. Fig 4A shows the absorption peaks of N-doped C-dots/TMB solution after exposing outdoor in a sunny day or a cloudy day for 5 min. After placed in a sunny day for 5 min, the solution of N-doped C-dots/TMB solution changed for colorless to an apparent green color. The green color is due to the mixture of blue (one-electron oxidation) product and yellow product (two-electron oxidation) of TMB [32]. In comparison, only slight green color was observed if the solution was placed in a cloudy day. This suggests the N-doped C-dots/TMB system can distinguish the UV radiation dose in different weather condition.

Fig. 4B shows the absorbance changes of N-doped C-dots/TMB solution after placing outdoor or indoor in a sunny day for 5 min. After exposing in the room (incandescent lighting is on) for 5 min, the N-doped C-dots/TMB solution is colorless. The solution even kept colorless for a long time (at least half a day) if it was placed indoor. The result demonstrates that the N-doped C-dots/TMB could effectively distinguish the light from sunlight or from the lamp in the room.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The smart wearable device for UV monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: In order to develop wearable device for UV dose monitoring, N-doped C-dots and TMB were integrated into agarose hydrogels. Fig. 5 shows the color changes of N-doped C-dots/TMB loaded hydrogels under the irradiation of 254, 300, and 350 nm. The oxidation of TMB by N-doped C-dots is still highly dependent on the radiation dose when loaded in gels. The color change of TMB provides a simple tool to quantify the dose of UV radiation in a wide range of wavelength, especially the quantification of UVA radiation was firstly achieved through the color change of TMB. The data suggest that the TMB/N-doped C-dots system have great potential application in UV dose quantification.

To demonstrate the practical application, a wearable bracelet for UV monitoring was fabricated by attaching the hydrogels onto a paper. As shown in Fig. 6 , the biocompatible paper based bracelet can directly wear on the wrist. The bracelet contains two separate gels, one gel is loaded with N-doped C-dots/TMB as the indicator, the other gel is only loaded with TMB as the reference. The wearable bracelet can be used for monitoring the dose of UV exposure outdoor in a sunny day. With the increasing exposure time, the N-doped C-dots/TMB loaded hydrogel gradually changed from colorless to green color in sunlight. The increases of UV dose exposure could be clearly observed by naked eyes. To quantify the color change, the color change of TMB in the bracelet can be simply analyzed using a smartphone with color recognition APP. As a control, the pictures of the bracelet exposed indoor (sunny day) or cloudy (outdoor) were also shown in Fig. S8. When the bracelet was exposed indoor, no color change was observed in 5 min. When the bracelet was placed outdoor in a cloudy day, only slight color was observed after 5 min exposing in sunlight. All these data demonstrate that the wearable bracelet has great potential for the UV exposure monitoring in outdoor activities.

As a well-known catalyst for TMB oxidation, nanozyme was used for monitoring the dose of UV exposure for the first time. Although the self-oxidation of TMB induced by UVB and UVC was recently reported, the introducing of N-doped C-dots as a catalyst to the photo-oxidation of TMB is of important value. First, the use of nanozyme as a catalyst can extend the response range of UV light, the N-doped C-dots /TMB system could be used for the monitoring the dose of UVA. Second, after the introducing of N-doped C-dots, the UV-induced TMB oxidation can work well in a near neutral pH. The limitation is that the color change of the wearable device is not reversible, so it can only be used as a disposable device. The development of re-usable wearable device based on the nanozyme-catalyzed reaction is still a challenge in the future.

Guofen ZHOU: Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Linlin YU: Validation, Data curation. Wenlong GUO: Writing – review & editing. Zhongwei JIANG: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Lianzhe HU: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Min WANG: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 22204011), Chongqing Natural Science Foundation (cstc2021jcyj-msxmX0757), and the Chongqing Innovation Research Group Project (no. CXQT21015).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.cjac.2024.100377.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UV-Sensitive Wearable Devices for Colorimetric Monitoring of UV Exposure

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A Paper-Based Wearable Photodetector for Simultaneous UV Intensity and Dosage Measurement

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A chemosensing approach for the colorimetric and spectroscopic detection of Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Gd3+ metal ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Mohan, T. Xing, S. Kumar, S. Kumar, S. Ma, F. Sun, D. Xing, P. Ren, A chemosensing approach for the colorimetric and spectroscopic detection of Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Gd3+ metal ions, Sci Total Environ 845(2022) 157242.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanistic Insight into Charge and Energy Transfers of Luminescent Metal–Organic Frameworks Based Sensors for Toxic Chemicals

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Mohan, S. Kumar, S. Ma, H. You, P. Ren, Mechanistic Insight into Charge and Energy Transfers of Luminescent Metal–Organic Frameworks Based Sensors for Toxic Chemicals, Adv Sustainable Syst 5(2021) 2000293.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Mohan, S. Ma, S. Kumar, Y. Yang, P. Ren, Tactile Sensors: Hydroxyl Decorated Silver Metal−Organic Frameworks for Detecting Cr2O72−, MnO4−, Humic Acid, and Fe3+ Ions, ACS Appl Mater Interfaces 15(2023) 17317–17323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanomaterials with enzyme-like characteristics (nanozymes): next-generation artificial enzymes (II)
